front: What is the precise mathematical definition of an interior point of a set A in a metric space (X, d)?
back: A point x ∈ A is called an interior point of A if there exists some ε > 0 such that the open ball B(x, ε) is entirely contained within A. That is, B(x, ε) ⊆ A.

front: Define the interior of a set A in a metric space.
back: The interior of a set A, denoted by A° or Int(A), is the set of all interior points of A. It is the largest open set contained within A.

front: What is the relationship between the interior of a set and open sets?
back: The interior of any set A is the union of all open sets contained in A. Consequently, the interior A° is itself an open set. A set is open if and only if it is equal to its own interior (A is open ⇔ A = A°).

front: How can the interior of a set be characterized using the complement and the closure operator?
back: The interior of a set A can be found by taking the complement of the closure of its complement. Symbolically: Int(A) = (Cl(Aᶜ))ᶜ. This shows the interior and closure are dual concepts.

front: Provide a basic example: In the standard metric space ℝ, what is the interior of the closed interval [a, b]?
back: The interior of the closed interval [a, b] is the open interval (a, b). This is because for any point x where a < x < b, we can find an ε-ball contained within [a,b], but at the endpoints a and b, every ε-ball will contain points outside the interval.
